During a recent SAP Datasphere reporting requirement, I needed to present quantities in a consistent unit of measure even though the source data was stored in different units. Rather than implementing custom conversion logic, I explored how SAP standard unit conversion tables could be leveraged through the CONVERT_UNIT function.

 

In SAP Datasphere, data frequently originates from multiple source systems that use different units of measure. To ensure accurate analytics and reporting, these quantities often need to be converted into a common business unit. In this example, the requirement is to convert quantities from LB to KG using SAP standard unit conversion logic.

 

This blog explains the SAP standard tables involved in unit conversion, the implementation approach in SAP Datasphere, the validation process, and the key lessons learned during the exercise.

 

Business Use Case:

Consider a manufacturing company that receives material quantities from suppliers in LB, while corporate reporting standards require all quantities to be reported in KG.

Without a standard conversion mechanism, reports from different locations become difficult to compare. SAP Datasphere's unit conversion capability helps standardize these quantities using SAP-maintained conversion factors and dimensions.

For example:

  • Source system stores quantity as 568 LB
  • Business users want reporting output in KG
  • Manual conversion in every report can lead to inconsistency
  • A centralized conversion logic in SAP Datasphere gives consistent and reusable results

This type of conversion is commonly needed in:

  • Inventory reporting
  • Procurement analytics
  • Material movement reporting
  • Cross-country operational reporting
  • Consolidated quantity reporting across multiple source systems

 

Prerequisites to understand:

Before implementing unit conversion in SAP Datasphere, it is important to understand the SAP standard tables used for unit conversion.

The two primary SAP standard tables involved in unit conversion are:

  • T006D – Dimension table
  • T006 – Unit of measurement table

Because these tables are maintained centrally within SAP, they provide a consistent and trusted source of conversion factors across applications and reporting scenarios.

 

T006D (Dimensions):

This table stores unit dimensions. DIMID is a primary key in this table, and it represents the ‘Dimension’, and MSSIE stores one unique SI/base unit for that dimension.

For example, for the dimension MASS, the SI/base unit is KG.

So, when we convert from LB to KG, both units belong to the same dimension: MASS.

This is important because unit conversion should happen only between units that belong to the same logical dimension.

 

Once the dimension and base unit have been identified in T006D, the next step is to understand how individual units within that dimension are maintained in T006.

 

T006 (Units of Measurement):

T006 stores the units of measurement and their conversion factors.

For the MASS dimension, units such as G, KG, OZ, LB, and TON can be maintained.

 

How to Implement Unit Conversion in Datasphere:

  1. Create a simple Fact View on the source table, for example ACDOCA, and enable the required fields.  In my current implementation, the source quantity field is MSL and the source unit field is RUNIT.
  2. Create 2 calculated columns in the ‘Formula’ node
  • MSL_Converted – to convert quantity
  • RUNIT_Converted – to display the converted target unit

 

 

Use the CONVERT_UNIT function to convert the source quantity from LB to KG.

In this example, the target unit is hardcoded as KG for simplicity.

However, this logic is suitable only for a simple demo scenario. In real projects, avoid hardcoding too many unit mappings in calculated columns. Instead, it is better to use an Input Parameter for the target unit, if business users need flexibility to convert quantities into different units such as KG, G, or TON

 

 

To show the converted unit in the output, create another calculated column.

 

This makes the output easier for business users to understand because the converted quantity and converted unit are shown together.

 

After deploying the view, perform a data preview and validate the converted result.

Manual Data Validation:

 

 

For the LB unit, the important fields used in this example are:

Field

Meaning

Value in this example

MSEHI

Unit of measure

LB

DIMID

Dimension

MASS

ZAEHL

Numerator

453592

NENNR

Denominator

1000000

EXP10

Power of 10 scaling factor

0

 

Here Quantity in Source Unit (ACDOCA-MSL) = 568

                                                           Source Unit = LB

                                                            Target Unit = KG

From T006:

ZAEHL = 453592

NENNR = 1000000

EXP10 = 0

 

 

The LB to KG conversion factor is derived as:

 

Quantity in Target Unit = Quantity in Source Unit * (ZAEHL / NENNR)

Quantity in Target Unit   = 568*(453592/1000000)

Quantity in Target Unit = 568 *(0.453592)

Quantity in Target Unit = 257.640256 KG

This matches the value returned by the CONVERT_UNIT function, so the conversion is verified and correct.

Note: SAP also stores an additional field called EXP10 (exponent), which is used for scaling in some unit conversions.
In this example, EXP10 = 0, so it does not impact the calculation.

 

Area

What to Check

Why It Matters

Source unit

Ensure the source unit value exists in T006

If the source unit is missing or invalid, conversion can fail

Dimension

Ensure source and target units belong to the same dimension

LB and KG belong to MASS, so conversion is valid

Conversion factor

Validate ZAEHL, NENNR, and EXP10

These fields determine the actual conversion factor

Target unit

Avoid unnecessary hardcoding

Hardcoding is okay for demos, but parameters are better for reusable models

Error handling

Use appropriate error handling based on requirement

fail on error helps identify missing or incorrect unit conversion data early

Validation

Always compare Datasphere output with manual calculation

This builds confidence before using the logic in reports

 

When Is Unit Conversion Needed?

Unit conversion is useful when the same business measure is captured in different units across systems or processes.

Some common scenarios are:

  • Source data stores quantity in vendor-specific units
  • Reporting requires a standard unit across countries or plants
  • Different SAP or non-SAP systems send different units
  • Planning and analytics require normalized quantities
  • Business users compare material quantities across regions

For example, if one plant stores inventory in LB and another plant reports in KG, the analytical output becomes inconsistent unless the quantities are converted into a common unit.

 

Common Pitfall:

One common issue is attempting to convert between units that belong to different dimensions. For example, SAP will not perform a valid conversion between MASS and LENGTH dimensions because their measurement contexts are unrelated. Therefore, validating the DIMID relationship in T006D should be one of the first troubleshooting steps when conversion results are unexpected.

Another common issue is hardcoding conversion factors instead of using SAP standard conversion logic. While manual calculations may work for simple examples, they can become difficult to maintain when conversion definitions change or additional units need to be supported.

 

Lessons Learned:

During this implementation, I observed the following:

  • Always verify that source and target units belong to the same dimension in T006D.
  • Validate Datasphere results using T006 conversion factors before deploying to production.
  • INPUT PARAMETERS provide more flexibility than hardcoded target units.
  • EXP10 should be reviewed for conversions involving scaling factors.
  • CONVERT_UNIT is preferable to manual calculations because it leverages SAP standard conversion logic.

 

Conclusion:

In this exercise, I used SAP standard tables T006 and T006D together with the CONVERT_UNIT function to convert quantities from LB to KG in SAP Datasphere.

 

The converted value was validated against SAP-maintained conversion factors, confirming the accuracy of the result. By relying on SAP's standard conversion framework instead of custom formulas, organizations can build more scalable, reusable, and reliable reporting solutions when working with multiple units of measure.

 

I hope this example helps you understand not only how unit conversion works in SAP Datasphere, but also how SAP standard tables can be used to validate and troubleshoot conversion results effectively.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June

June was a full month. Three releases shipped back to back, the team delivered across a wide range of areas, and the German SAP user group DSAG kept the conversations grounded with honest feedback and real customer examples at their AK Business Analytics meeting in St. Leon-Rot.

Learn more in my top features video in June 2026 on YouTube 📺.

In addition, explore the latest updates and blogs in my community news blog.

 

My top features in June

Use Object Store Sources in Replication Flows

You can now use object store sources directly in replication flows. Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Gen2, Google Cloud Storage, SFTP, HANA Data Lake Files, and Microsoft OneLake are all supported. The data lands in local table files in Datasphere. If your raw data lives in cloud storage, you no longer need a separate ingestion pipeline.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Oracle as a Source in Replication Flows

Oracle is now supported as a source in replication flows, with Initial Only load type. Full snapshot replication from Oracle to your supported targets in Datasphere. Oracle Wallet authentication is also now supported, enabling SSL and certificate-based authentication via mTLS.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Snowflake as a Source in Replication Flows

You can now create Snowflake connections and use them as a source in replication flows, also with Initial Only load type. For non-public Snowflake environments, Cloud Connector is supported as a reverse invoke proxy, routing traffic through a TLS tunnel.

➡️  More information

 

 

Cloud Connector Support for Confluent Cloud Connections

You can now configure Cloud Connector for Confluent Cloud connections. Same principle as Snowflake: Cloud Connector routes traffic through a TLS tunnel so replication flows can reach Confluent Cloud without public endpoints. Requires Cloud Connector to be set up in your landscape.

➡️  More information

 

 

SQL View Transform in Transformation Flows on File

You can now create a SQL view transform inside a transformation flow on file, using Apache Spark SQL. A full SQL editor with auto-complete, drag-and-drop source objects, real-time validation, and data preview. All of this running directly in your file space. This capability was explicitly listed as not supported until this release.

➡️  More information

 

 

Direct Delta Sharing Client Integration for Transformation Flows

Accessing delta share tables in transformation flows, from BDC Foundation Services or Databricks, no longer requires an intermediate step. The integration is now direct, with no intermediate copying, better performance, and reduced storage overhead.

 

 

CLI Extensions: Pause/Resume Replication Flows + File Space Import/Export

Two CLI improvements in one release. You can now pause and resume replication flows and individual replication objects via the command line. File space objects are also now accessible from the CLI: import and export of spaces, modeling objects, tasks, and connectivity. Fewer things that require manual UI interaction for teams running Datasphere through automation.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enhanced Capacity Unit Monitoring

The Capacity Unit Monitoring tool now has a customizable date and time range filter. No longer limited to the current month. A new Spaces tab shows consumption broken down per space, covering Premium Outbound Integration, object store usage, and total capacity unit consumption. Useful for anyone managing multi-space environments.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enable System Notifications ⭐ KP's Pick

You can now configure email notifications for replication flow and task chain completions directly in your user profile settings, for success, failure, or both. No more manual monitor checking to find out if an overnight run completed. Small feature, real impact.

➡️  More information

 

 

YOUTUBE LINK

 
 

DSAG AK Business Analytics — June Meeting in St. Leon-Rot

On June 19 I joined the DSAG AK Business Analytics working group in St. Leon-Rot for their summer session. These meetings have a specific quality that's hard to find elsewhere. The audience is technical, the questions are direct, and nobody is there to be sold to.

The agenda this time was particularly strong:

▶️  SAP Business Data Cloud roadmap including fresh SAPPHIRE announcements (Fabian Hartje)
▶️  SAP Analytics Cloud roadmap update (Matthias Kraemer)
▶️  AI in practice: how Swisscom uses AI in the SAP ecosystem (Tjarko von Lehsten, Viktor Seide)
▶️  BDC Object Store implementation with real-world examples (Stefan Härtelt)
▶️  360° BDC — Data Provisioning, SAC and Planning, Parts I and II (Maria Alejandra Uruena, Sandy Tran, Jie Deng)

Fresh SAPPHIRE updates, live demos, and real customer examples in one room. That's a good day. Thanks to AK speakers Jörg Steinhorst and Stefan Kahle for organizing, and to everyone who showed up and contributed to the discussion.

 

My favorite blogs in June

SAP Analytics Cloud is expanding live data connectivity beyond the classic live models. @david_stocker explains how BigQuery, Snowflake, and Azure SQL are already supported, with SharePoint and more cloud data lakes coming later this year. No replication, no import jobs. Just a live connection via Smart Data Access and virtual tables behind the scenes. The blog also explains how this underpins Seamless Planning. A good orientation read if you haven't looked at live data connectivity yet.
🔗 Live Data Connectivity in SAP Analytics Cloud - Overview and Direction

@AndreasForsterpublished a hands-on tutorial for vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with SAP Business Application Studio, Python, and an AI coding agent. Instead of writing the PAL/APL code yourself, you describe what you want and let the agent generate it. Jupyter notebooks and all. The example is a time-series forecast, but the setup works for any ML scenario HANA Cloud supports. He also shows how to add current documentation context to keep the generated code accurate.
🔗 Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline

CDC-based replication flows in Datasphere don't use ODP queues at all. @anilkumarauce traces the full internal architecture step by step: changes are captured at database table level using logging tables, triggers, and a staging layer called the Resilient Data Buffer, all the way from an S/4HANA application table into Datasphere. Exactly the kind of under-the-hood knowledge that saves hours when something unexpected happens in production.
🔗 Under the Hood of ABAP CDC in SAP Datasphere Replication Flows

The most complete provisioning reference for SAP Business Data Cloud on SAP Community. @ShreyasriB built out an 8-part step-by-step series covering the full provisioning journey: from the initial BDC Cockpit setup via SAP for Me through Datasphere, SAC, Databricks, HANA Cloud, Intelligent Apps, and Snowflake. The kind of guide you want open in a second browser tab while doing the actual setup.
🔗 Provisioning of Business Data Cloud: Start with Part 1

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • SAP Enterprise Planning: Where AI meets strategy and execution
  • Business Data Architecture: Modeling Layers for Structured Transformations
  • Upcoming Change: Member Limit Enforcement for Data Action Parameters
  • Managing Datasphere Consumption: Gaining Visibility and Control Over Capacity Units
  • Introducing Support Content — the additional Customer Support knowledge repository

  • Foundational Success Plan - SAP Enterprise Support Services and Guidance for SAP BDC and AI
  • Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline
  • Integrating SAP Business Data Cloud with SAP S/4HANA Cloud using Cloud Integration Automation
  • Why Business Meaning Matters in Modern Analytics Landscapes
  • Business Data Architecture for Modern Analytics Landscapes
  • Use Formations to Link SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere for Seamless Planning
  • Showcasing SAP Business Data Cloud - Demo options and use cases - Quarterly update
  • Provisioning of Business Data Cloud: SAP Snowflake
  • Joule with SAP Datasphere – Step by Step Setup Guide
  • Provisioning of Business Data Cloud: SAP Datasphere, Data Composer
  • The AI Isn't the Problem. The Data Underneath It Is.
  • Need to Know - Beyond SAP Analytics Cloud AI and Using SAP Databricks in SAP Business Data Cloud
  • From Fragmented Landscapes to Unified Insights: Business Consent in SAP Business Data Cloud
  • From "AI in Applications" to "AI on Applications"
  • SAP Datasphere: Data Tiering Strategy and Best Practices
  • S/4HANA Public Cloud in the Age of AI and Business Data Cloud

  • Live Versions for Planning Models in SAP Analytics Cloud: Microsoft Azure Is Here!
  • Accelerate Your Journey to the Autonomous Enterprise: Join Our Exclusive Partner Webinar Series
