  • From Fragmented Landscapes to Unified Insights: Business Consent in SAP BDC
  • Communicating the value of SAP Business Data Cloud using personas
  • Hands-on Tutorial: Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud and BAS
  • Leading with SAP Business Data Cloud innovation and roadmap
  • End-to-End Forecasting with SAP Business Data Cloud
 

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic pages for SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere. Also check out the SAP Help for SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere, including for troubleshooting and analysis guides, onboarding guides, and more.

entry.title: DSP Models used in SAC Stories (BW-like where-used)
entry.content:
Having difficulties to identify impact of failed DSP Loads to SAC Stories? 
Having difficulties to identify how many SAC Stories need to be adjusted with an DSP Model Dimension change for example? 
Looking for where-used like BW feature? Impact analyzes? Lineage from DSP to SAC. 

Manual Steps:
1. Get all Stories and all Models data into a JSON: https://<your-tenant>/api/v1/stories?include=models
2. Save the results into a file
3. Process the file with a parser

I used python and have had the script vibe-code generated: I asked: "create parser python script on an example (first Story from the JSON) with delimiter: "{"name":" to "{"name":" and output format for (for example) DevOps Wiki. Provide a List of Models for each Story (use models: and description:)_. Can be enhanced with: create link to SAC Story providing the base-link as well.

Can be automated, but... bearer token is needed to fetch the result using a notebook, can be downloaded from browser, but SAC has only 8 hours validity. Since the Story to Model is static, manual way should be enough.

Simple and efficient. API: /api/v1/stories?include=models helps not only to create dynamic jump links to another SAC Stories, but also can be used for semi-automated documentation

Example: 

{"name":"<STORY NAME>","description":"<STORY DESC>","createdBy":"<USER_ID>","id":"<STORY_ID>","created":"<TIMESTAMP>","changed":"<TIMESTAMP>","isTemplate":false,"isSample":false,"models":[{"description":"<MODEL1_ID>","id":"<MODEL1_INTERNAL_ID>","remoteConnection":{"connectionType":"DIRECT","systemType":"DWC","name":"<CONNECTION_ID>","protocol":"HTTPS","host":"<URL>","port":"443","id":"<CONNECTION_ID>","scpAccount":null},"isPlanning":false},{"description":"<MODEL2_ID>","id":"<MODEL2_INTERNAL_ID>","remoteConnection":{"connectionType":"DIRECT","systemType":"DWC","name":"<CONNECTION_ID>","protocol":"HTTPS","host":"<URL>","port":"443","id":"<CONNECTION_ID>","scpAccount":null},"isPlanning":false}],"changedBy":"<USER_ID>","openURL":"<URL>"},{"name":.... 

 

Main blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/bw-vs-datasphere-dsp-amp-sac/ba-p/1428...

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Custom ABAP CDS View to Datasphere: Replication Flow Setup, Status & Delta Processing
entry.content:
Introduction: 

This technical guide explains how to replicate data from a custom ABAP CDS view in SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere using Replication Flows with Change Data Capture (CDC). It covers the concepts, the end-to-end setup, how deltas are captured, the related jobs and system tables, and what to expect during initial and delta runs.
You’ll learn:
  • How RMS, APE, and CDC work together
  • How to prepare a CDC-enabled custom CDS view
  • How to configure a Replication Flow in Datasphere for Initial only and Initial + Delta loads
  • What artifacts (logging and buffer tables, jobs) are created in S/4HANA and how to monitor them
  • How to validate inserts, updates, and deletes end-to-end
  • SAP Datasphere local table, Internal delta capture columns (Change Date or Change Type).

Basics:

  • Replication Management Service (RMS): Orchestrates change data capture lifecycles, creates and monitors master/subscriber logging tables, and runs real-time jobs for observing and pushing deltas. Manages the end-to-end reliability of triggers and logging.
  • ABAP Pipeline Engine (APE): An ABAP-based runtime used by Datasphere to extract from ABAP sources (S/4HANA tables or CDC-enabled CDS views). Acts as the “consumer” in CDC terms for Replication Flows. Supporting Initial Only, Delta Only & Initial and Delta options.
  • Change Data Capture (CDC): Uses database triggers to record INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE changes on base tables that back an ABAP CDS view.Captured changes flow from: Base table triggers → Master Logging Table → Subscriber Logging Table → Buffer Table → Replication Flow into Datasphere target.

Prerequisites and Preparation:

Authorizations:
  • ABAP development access to create a CDS view
  • Access to SM37, DHADM, DHCDCMON, DHRDBMON for monitoring
  • Permissions in SAP Datasphere to create and run Replication Flows
System readiness:
  • RFC/ODP or ABAP connection from Datasphere to S/4HANA (as per your Datasphere connection setup)
  • Sufficient background work processes for real-time jobs
SAP S/4HANA RMS job settings, scheudling and operational tuning:

Standard jobs (auto-created/managed, visible in SM37; commonly run under DDIC)

  • /1DH/CDC_HEALTH_CHECK: Periodic CDC health verification.
  • SAP_DHCDC_DISPATCHER: Dispatcher for CDC orchestration.
  • /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB: Moves changes from Master Logging Table to Subscriber Logging Table. Default reschedule interval ~1 hour.
  • /1DH/PUSH_CDS_DELTA (aka /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA): Joins subscriber logs with base table and writes Buffer Table. Default reschedule interval ~6 hours.

Tuning guidance:

  • Start with defaults, then shorten intervals if your delta SLAs require it and your system resources allow.
  • Coordinate with Basis to adjust job class/priority and periodicity to avoid long-run job lists and ensure timely delta movement.
  • Monitor DHCDCMON (log table depth) and DHRDBMON (buffer latency). If backlogs build, increase job frequency and/or background work processes.

Connectivity:

  • ABAP or SAP S/4HANA Connection between SAP Datasphere and SAP S/4HANA.
  • SAP Datasphere + SAP S/4HANA: Your Guide to Seamless Data Integration.
  • SAP Cloud Connector for SAP S/4HANA (Private/ On-prem).
Design recommendations: Prefer SAP standard released CDS views or SAP data products where possible for lifecycle stability. If not then create custom ABAP CDS view/entity, data products instead of using direct ABAP tables as context information is lost and significant effort is required to reverse-engineer the logic. 
  • SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

1. Create a CDC‑Enabled Custom CDS View

The example below shows a simplified custom ABAP CDS view Z_SALESORDER on top of a custom base table ZVBAK. It enables analytical usage and data extraction, and configures CDC mapping so deltas are captured.

Sample reference code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZISDSALESORDER'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@EndUserText.label: 'Z Sales Order'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel: {
   compositionRoot:   true,
   representativeKey: 'SalesOrder',
   usageType: {
     dataClass:      #TRANSACTIONAL,
     serviceQuality: #B,
     sizeCategory:   #L
   },
   supportedCapabilities: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION, #CDS_MODELING_ASSOCIATION_TARGET, #SQL_DATA_SOURCE, #CDS_MODELING_DATA_SOURCE, #EXTRACTION_DATA_SOURCE ],
   modelingPattern: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION ]
}
@ObjectModel.sapObjectNodeType.name: 'SalesOrder'

@Analytics: {
    dataExtraction: {
        enabled: true,
        delta.changeDataCapture: {
            mapping:[
                {
                    table: 'zvbak', role: #MAIN,
                    viewElement: ['SalesOrder'],
                    tableElement: ['vbeln']  }
            ]
        }
    }
 }
define view Z_SALESORDER as select from zvbak
//Association
  association [0..*]...{<Columns>};

For testing, I developed a custom ABAP program to perform CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE operations on the ZVBAK custom table and validate end‑to‑end propagation. I initially inserted 10 records.

Use transaction SE16N on the CDS SQL view ZISDSALESORDER to view the record count.

2. Create a Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere.

Replication Flows are the recommended mechanism for high‑volume replication into SAP Datasphere and supported targets.

2.1 Note: If you choose Initial Only mode, the target will have the same columns as the source (130 columns in this case).

2.2 When using the Initial and Delta mode, SAP Datasphere adds two internal columns (Change_Type and Change_Date) to the Z_SALESORDER local table. The following Change_Type values are typically observed in ABAP CDS replication. Replication Flow Scenarios with Different Load Types.

OperationChange_Type
Initial Load L
Pure Insert I
Delta Load (UPSERT: insert if not exists, else update by key )A 
Delta Load (DELETE)D 

Note: The delta capture table is an internal SAP Datasphere object whose structure may change at any time and is not released for external access.It is only used by SAP Datasphere internal applications (for example, Transformation Flows, Replication Flows etc.)Using the internal delta capture columns (Change_Date or Change_Type) or their content for any external delta replication outside Premium Outbound Integration is not permitted. 

3725855 - Delta Columns CHANGE_TYPE and CHANGE_DATE not visible in Data Viewer or Data Products for Local Tables in HDLF Spaces in SAP Datasphere

3634750 - Unexpected behavior with delta enabled Datasphere local table and models using the same feature

2.3  The Replication Flow’s delta frequency is configured for every 5 minutes (default: 1 hour). You can modify this as required. Using 0 hours and 0 minutes switches to near real‑time.

 

3. Execute the Replication Flow for the first time to perform the Initial Load

3.1 In DHADM, you can see that a new subscriber and buffer table are created in SAP S/4HANA for CDS Z_SALESORDER (check via T‑Codes DHADM and DHRDBMON). This occurs when you run the Replication Flow for the first time. Subscriber ID: C30227C32B841FE19EF81806E972D0BE; Producer Type: CDC; Consumer Type: APE (ABAP Pipeline Engine).

3.2 Master Loggining Table /1DH/ML000000024 and Subscriber Logging table /1DH/SL000000024 got created and can also be monitored in the Transaction DHCDCMON.

Master Logging Table: /1DH/ML<XXXXXXXXX>, It captures delta records from triggers as the single source of truth.

  • Structure: Includes the base table’s key fields and a sequence key (/1DH/SEQUENCE).
  • Quantity: One Master Logging Table per CDC mapping in the CDS view. For example, two CDC mappings generate two Master Logging Tables.

Subscriber Logging Table: /1DH/SL<XXXXXXXXX>, It is populated by the real‑time job /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB when the Master Logging Table has records and Mirrors the Master Logging Table but uses the base table’s key fields.

3.3 Resilient Data Buffer Table: /1DH/RDB0000HK: /1DH/RDB<XXXXX>, It is a transient staging framework / buffer table in the ABAP system that acts as a temporary staging area between S/4HANA and SAP Datasphere, ensuring deltas are safely staged, acknowledged, and only deleted after successful delivery. You can monitor them via transactions DHRDBMON (S/4HANA) or LTRDBMON (DMIS-based systems).

  • For each CDS view + replication flow combination, the ABAP Pipeline Engine/APRS creates one or more RDB tables as buffer objects.  One CDS view + one replication flow → one RDB table. Same CDS view + second replication flow → another RDB table for that flow.
  • The final structure matching the CDS view; populated by the real‑time job /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA and Mirrors the CDS view fields; /1DH/SEQUENCE serves as the key.
  • /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA joins the Subscriber Logging Table with the base table to fill the Buffer Table, ensuring only delta records are forwarded to the target.

3.4 As shown below, the Initial Load has completed, and the Replication Flow is retrying deltas every 5 minutes as configured.

 

3.5 In SAP Datasphere, preview the Z_SALESORDER table; the Change_Type = 'L' after initial load is completed and Change_Date was updated when the records were inserted.

 

4. Execute the Replication Flow: Delta Load with INSERT, UPDATE, MODIFY and DELETE Scenarios

4.1 Using the ABAP program, INSERT 2 new sales orders into ZVBAK. In SE16N (ZISDSALESORDER), you’ll see 12 records, and DHRDBMON shows 2 new entries as Current records.

 

 

Buffer Table /1DH/RDB0000HK-/1DH/OPERATION value is U means “UPSERT” (used for both INSERT and UPDATE operations in the RDB buffer)

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load frequency interval elapses.

You’ll notice that the two new orders (5011, 5012) have Change_Type = “A” (upsert: insert if not present, or update by primary key if it exists), whereas “L” denotes the initial load (records loaded during the first full load).

 

4.2 UPDATE 3 existing sales order: 5004, 5005,5006.

You’ll notice that for orders 5004, 5005, and 5006, Change_Type changed from “L” (Initial Load) to “A” (Upsert).

4.3 DELETE three existing orders: 5006, 5007, and 5008.

In the buffer table /1DH/RDB0000HK, the /1DH/OPERATION value is “D” for DELETE operations

In the SAP Datasphere local table Z_SALESORDER, deleted records appear with Change_Type = “D.” On the source side (SAP S/4HANA table ZVBAK and the CDS view Z_SALESORDER), those records were removed from the database. This confirms that Replication Flows with CDC in SAP Datasphere capture all changes, CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE.

5.High‑Level Flow for ABAP CDS View Replication into SAP Datasphere

On the first execution of a Replication Flow, the system creates the subscriber tables, RDB buffer tables, and logging tables. These tables are generated per object + replication flow combination.

The Initial Load transfers data from the source CDS view to SAP Datasphere, and the corresponding Change_Type = Lin the Datasphere local table.

For delta loads, CDC database triggers capture CRUD changes on the base table and write them to the Master Logging Table.

  • Master Logging Table: Stores delta records with /1DH/SEQUENCE as the key.
  • Observer Job ( /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB ) Moves entries from the Master Logging Table to the Subscriber Logging Table.
  • Subscriber Logging Table: Holds delta records keyed by the base table’s keys.
  • Push Delta Job ( /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA ) Joins subscriber logs with the base table to prepare records for extraction.
  • Buffer Table: Provides the final CDS-aligned structure for Datasphere to ingest into the target defined in the Replication Flow.

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load interval has elapsed.

After the initial load, for delta processing:

  • Create/Update operations are represented as Change_Type = A
  • Delete operations are represented as Change_Type = D

 

Conclusion:

Replication Flows with CDC provide a reliable way to move CDS-based data from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere with clear handling for initial loads, updates, inserts, and deletes. During the first run, the full dataset is loaded into the target, and during delta processing the runtime automatically captures source changes through triggers, logging tables, and the resilient data buffer before delivering them to Datasphere. In the target local table, the Change_Type values help validate the lifecycle of each record: L for initial load, A for upsert-style delta changes, and D for deletions. This makes it easier to monitor data movement end to end and confirm that the source and target remain synchronized.

 

References:

  1. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE 
  2. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/replication-flow-blog-series-part-1-overvi... 
  3. https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/datasphere/best-practices-troubleshooting 
  4. https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4428871866.html 

Happy Learning!😊

entry.title: Architecture Deep-Dive: Transforming SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Architecture Deep-Dive: Transforming SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud

As organizations navigate complex enterprise modernization, the pressure to transition legacy data warehouses into agile frameworks has intensified. However, completely tearing down years—or decades—of structural investments in SAP BW is rarely a viable or cost-effective option.

The strategic alternative is a phased modernization journey leveraging SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) to implement a comprehensive Business Data Fabric & Data Mesh Approach. By adopting the Optimize, Modernize, and Innovate framework, enterprise architects can systematically evolve an existing SAP BW system into an agile, AI-driven data fabric without disrupting live operations.

📊Modernization Blueprint: The Optimize-Modernize-Innovate Journey

Part I: The Sequential Core Technical Pillars

Phase 1: Optimize – Reducing Footprints & Preparing the Landscape

The journey begins by stabilizing the current warehouse estate and tackling infrastructure overhead:

  • Step 1.1: Data Offloading: Cold, non-critical, or historical data that is rarely accessed for active operational reporting is offloaded from the transactional environment directly into the Object Store (SAP HANA Data Lake Files). This action effectively decouples storage capacity from active compute resources, dramatically optimizing infrastructure run costs.

  • Step 1.2: System Move (As-is): Existing installations of SAP BW NetWeaver 7.5 or SAP BW/4HANA are migrated "as-is" into 2a. SAP BW, private cloud edition. This move preserves data structures, custom logic, and transformations without demanding an immediate, high-risk reconstruction of legacy assets.

Phase 2: Modernize – Shifting to a Business Data Fabric

Once optimized, data structures transition from isolated warehouse tables into accessible enterprise data products:

  • Step 2.1: Create/Activate Data Subscription: Utilizing the native Data Product Generator tool, architects create data subscriptions to extract info from legacy BW InfoProviders into dedicated Local Tables within an isolated SAP BW Space inside 2b. SAP Datasphere.

  • Step 2.2: Run Merge Task: Incremental data updates land inside an inbound delta buffer. A scheduled Merge Task (orchestrated via an SAP Datasphere task chain and triggered by SAP BW process chains) processes these delta components, continuously applying changes back into the local tables.

  • Step 2.3: Scenario Generator (Complex Modeling): For intricate, multi-layered historical models, the Scenario Generator helps automatically resolve and mass-generate multiple data subscriptions while preserving strict semantic dependencies.

  • Step 2.4: Create Custom Data Product: Using the Data Sharing Cockpit, raw technical data models are contextualized and exposed as governed Custom Data Products listed inside the central 2e. Catalog & Marketplace.

Phase 3: Innovate – Zero-Copy Advanced AI & Closed-Loop Analytics

The final phase leverages this modernized fabric to power advanced business intelligence and machine learning pipelines:

  • Step 3.1: Share Data Product: Rather than creating redundant physical copies of massive data sets, data products from the Marketplace are securely shared instantly with 2d. SAP Databricks via Zero-copy data sharing.

  • Step 3.2: Advanced AI/ML in Databricks: Data lands in the Databricks Unity Catalog as shared delta tables, where data scientists use ML Notebooks for advanced data engineering and predictive forecasting.

  • Step 3.3: Publish New Data Product: The downstream intelligence generated inside Databricks is packaged using the SAP Business Data Cloud SDK and published straight back into the central Marketplace as an enriched data product.

  • Step 3.4: Integrate Other Data Sources: New, cloud-native data flows directly into 2b. SAP Datasphere, utilizing out-of-the-box, SAP-managed data products and pre-built intelligent content from SAP S/4HANA or SAP SuccessFactors to support clean core principles.

  • Step 3.5: Combine Data & Model: Create views and analytic models within SAP Datasphere to combine historic BW data (via custom data products) with new SAP-managed data products for holistic views.

  • Step 3.6: Reporting & Analysis in SAC: Finely tuned analytical models are exposed directly to 2c. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for unified corporate reporting and real-time insights.

  • Step 3.7: Seamless Planning Data Entry: SAC dashboard planners can execute data entries that write planning logic and allocations natively back into the underlying Datasphere local tables, enabling closed-loop hybrid planning.

Part II: Real-World Architectural Scenarios

The transformation framework natively accommodates four distinct deployment methodologies depending on the lifecycle of specific functional areas:

  • 🏢Scenario A: Preserve Historic Data & Replace BW (Yellow Path): Historic BW InfoProviders are pushed via the Data Product Generator into custom data products. In parallel, new analytical pipelines are established directly from operational sources. Both streams are blended seamlessly within Datasphere views and surface in SAC. This allows the structured, gradual decommissioning of the legacy on-premises SAP BW core system over time without losing access to historical data.

  • 🌿Scenario B: Greenfield Approach (New Sources, Clean Core) (Green Path): For entirely new implementations or fresh source systems (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors or cloud-native SAP S/4HANA instances), the framework skips the private cloud edition entirely. Data flows directly through out-of-the-box, SAP-managed data products and pre-built intelligent content inside BDC.

  • 📐Scenario C: Extended Greenfield Scenario (Cyan Path): When SAP-managed data products do not provide the entirety of necessary bespoke business logic, developers create custom semantic layers directly within Datasphere views and Analytic Models. Traditional staging tiers are fully replaced with native cloud views.

  • 📊Scenario 😧 Seamless Planning (Hybrid) (Magenta Path): This pathway connects planning loops between the analytical surface and the data fabric. Dashboard allocations made in SAC write back natively to Datasphere local tables. Furthermore, existing legacy BPC planning data models can be ingested and combined into these models.

Part III: Commercial Prerequisite Deep-Dive

Beyond the purely technical merits, this architecture addresses the long-standing problem of rigid software licensing. Under the SAP Business Data Cloud framework, all underlying services fall under a unified cloud contract driven by Capacity Units (CUs). Organizations can dynamically allocate and shift these units across the entire stack in self-service mode.

However, when architecting your BDC deployment blueprint, you must factor in the strict minimum baseline requirement enforced by the platform:

  • Core Tenant Provisioning Requirement: Enforces a strict hard floor minimum of 1,532 Capacity Units per month explicitly to provision and spin up a base tenant of SAP Datasphere (providing the baseline compute and disk storage resources required to run the environment).

If you offload massive volumes of data to the Object Store and downsize your active private cloud BW footprint, those exact Capacity Units are immediately freed up. You can instantly pivot those units to power your high-compute Databricks AI modeling nodes or expand user seating inside SAP Analytics Cloud—all without changing your overarching subscription framework.

References

https://help.sap.com/docs/business-data-cloud/administering-sap-business-data-cloud/administering-sap-business-data-cloud

https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-sap-business-data-cloud?searchId=99475d07-220d-49ea-a5ad-82bbf9972c67&listPosition=1

#SAP #SAPDatasphere #SAPAnalyticsCloud #SAPBDC #SAPBTP #SAPBW #BW4HANA #CloudMigration #Databricks #DataEngineering #AI #MachineLearning

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Seamless Planning入門 - Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう
entry.content:
このブログシリーズは、SAP Business Data CloudのSeamless Planningの全体像を一通り把握していただくことを目的としています。

このシリーズは、以下で構成されます。
- Part1 : Seamless Planningとは
- Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう（本ブログ）
- Part3 : Seamless PlanningでSAP Datasphereの実績ビューをライブバージョンで参照しよう
- to be continued ...

本ブログ「Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう」では、実際にSeamless Planningモデルを作成する手順と、作成後にSAP Datasphere側でデータを確認する方法を解説します。
 

目次

1. Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成
2. ファクトテーブルをSAP Datasphereに公開
3. SAP Datasphereでデータを確認

1. Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成

SAP Analytics Cloudの計画モデルはデータ保存先を「SAP Analytics Cloud」もしくは「SAP Datasphere」に選択することができます。SAP Datasphereを選択することでSeamless Planningの機能をご利用いただくことができます。

手順：

1.1. SAP Analytics Cloudのナビゲーションバーから 「モデラ」＞新規作成「モデル」＞ 「次へ」を選択します。（今回は「空のモデルから開始」の場合をご案内します）

 1.2. データ保管場所として「SAP Datasphere」を選択し、使用したいスペースを選択して「作成」をクリックします。

1.3.  以降はメジャーやディメンションの設定を行い、モデルを保存します。

これでまずはSAP Datasphereにデータが保管されるSAP Analytics Cloudの計画モデルが作成できました。

 

2. ファクトテーブルをSAP Datasphereに公開

 

SAP Analytics Cloud上で計画モデルを作成・保存しただけでは、SAP DatasphereのData Builder上からファクトテーブルを参照することができません。SAP Analytics Cloud側から「公開」操作を行う必要があります。この操作によって、SAP DatasphereのモデラがSAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをビューや分析モデルのソースとして利用できるようになります。

手順：

2.1. SAP Analytics Cloudで1で作成した計画モデルを開き、右側のモデル詳細パネルにて「SAP Datasphereでの公開」＞「編集...」をクリックします。

 
2.2. 「SAP Datasphereでデータファンデーショnをファクトテーブルとして...1」にチェックが入ってることを確認し、ファクトテーブルの名前を入力して「OK」をクリックします 。

2.3. モデルを保存します。

 これでSAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをSAP Datasphereでも使用できるようになりました。

3. SAP Datasphereでデータを確認

SAP Datasphere側にファクトテーブルが格納されているかを確認してみましょう。

手順：

3.1. SAP Analytics Cloudの右上「製品切り替え」よりSAP Datasphereに切り替えます。

 3.2. ナビゲーションバーの「データビルダ」より対象のスペースを選択すると、2で公開したファクトテーブルを確認することができました。

SAP Datasphereにて、SAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをビューや分析モデルのソースとして利用できるようになります。 

 

以上、Seamless Planningモデルの作成とSAP Datasphereでのデータ確認方法を解説しました。次回のブログでは、SAP Datasphereのビューをライブバージョンとして計画モデルに接続し、実績データをリアルタイム参照する方法を解説します。

参考資料・ブログ

- Expose Objects for Consumption in SAP Datasphere | SAP Help Portal
- Seamless Planning with Datasphere & SAC: Seamless Planning in the Cloud

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Architecture: Datasphere, Databricks, and the Unified Data Layer
entry.content:
This blog covers the technical architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) — focusing on the integration between SAP Datasphere, Databricks, and SAP Analytics Cloud. It is written for data architects and technical leads designing data platform strategy on a modern SAP landscape.

Applies to: SAP Business Data Cloud (GA as of 2024). SAP Datasphere (all current releases). SAP Analytics Cloud. Databricks (Unity Catalog enabled). This article references the jointly engineered SAP-Databricks integration — not the legacy SAP HANA Databricks connector.

1. BDC architectural layers — how they connect

SAP Business Data Cloud operates across three interconnected layers. Understanding the technical responsibility of each layer is essential for architecture planning:

 
LayerComponentPrimary FunctionKey Objects
Semantic & GovernanceSAP DatasphereBusiness meaning, data federation, access controlSpaces, Business Layer, Data Access Controls, Replication Flows
Compute & MLDatabricks (Unity Catalog)Lakehouse storage, ML model training, advanced analyticsDelta Lake tables, ML Experiments, Feature Store, Unity Catalog
Analytical UXSAP Analytics CloudBI, planning, predictive analyticsStories, Models, Analytic Applications, Planning Models
Business ContentSAP Content PackagesPre-built data products for business domainsFinance Data Product, Supply Chain Data Product, HR Data Product
 

2. Datasphere configuration for BDC

Datasphere acts as the semantic and governance layer in BDC. Before connecting to Databricks, Datasphere must be configured with the correct Space structure and replication flows from SAP source systems.

Space architecture for BDC

Design one Datasphere Space per business domain. Each Space is a governed, isolated data workspace with its own access controls:

Recommended Datasphere Space structure for BDC

Datasphere Tenant ├─ Space: FIN (Finance domain) │ ├─ Replication Flow: S/4HANA Finance → FIN (via SAP HANA Cloud or ODP) │ ├─ Data Layer: ACDOCA, BSEG, FAGLFLEXT (raw replication) │ ├─ Business Layer: Finance Fact View, Cost Centre Dimension, Profit Centre Dimension │ └─ Data Access Control: Finance_Users (row-level by Company Code) ├─ Space: SCM (Supply Chain) │ └─ (same pattern: replicate → model → govern) └─ Space: HR └─ (additional GDPR Data Access Controls for personal data)

Connecting Datasphere to Databricks

The BDC integration uses a jointly engineered connector between Datasphere and Databricks Unity Catalog. Configuration steps:

1. Create a Databricks connection in Datasphere

In Datasphere → Connections → New Connection → Databricks. Provide your Databricks workspace URL, personal access token (or service principal credentials), and HTTP path for the SQL warehouse.

2. Configure Unity Catalog metastore sharing

In Databricks Unity Catalog, create a catalog for SAP data (e.g. sap_business_data). Grant the Datasphere service principal USE CATALOG and CREATE TABLE privileges on this catalog.

3. Create data flows from Datasphere to Databricks

In Datasphere → Data Integration → Data Flows. Create a flow from your Business Layer view to the Databricks target catalog. Schedule for incremental replication using watermark-based CDC.

4. Validate semantic preservation in Databricks

Query the replicated table in Databricks. Verify that business-meaningful column names (not SAP technical field names like BUKRS) and calculated measures are preserved in the Delta table schema.

3. SAP Content Packages — pre-built data products

SAP provides pre-built business content packages for BDC that encode SAP's domain knowledge into governed data products. These reduce the time-to-value for common analytical use cases:

Content PackageSource SystemsKey Measures IncludedAI Use Cases Enabled
Finance Data ProductS/4HANA (FI/CO)Revenue, Cost, EBITDA, Cash Flow by dimensionFinancial forecasting, anomaly detection, close acceleration
Supply Chain Data ProductS/4HANA (MM/SD/PP)On-time delivery, inventory turns, OTIF, lead timeDemand forecasting, supply risk scoring, stock optimisation
HR Data ProductSuccessFactors, S/4HANA HRHeadcount, attrition rate, time-to-hire, engagementAttrition prediction, workforce planning, skills gap analysis
Procurement Data ProductS/4HANA (MM), AribaSpend by category, supplier performance, PO cycle timeSupplier risk scoring, spend analytics, contract compliance

4. ML model development on BDC data

With SAP business data available in Databricks via the BDC integration, ML models can be trained directly on semantically-rich, governed data:

 
Example: Demand forecasting model on BDC Supply Chain data (Python / MLflow)

import mlflow import pandas as pd from databricks.sdk.runtime import spark from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor # Read from BDC Supply Chain data product in Unity Catalog df = spark.table("sap_business_data.supply_chain.sales_order_history").toPandas() # Features derived from SAP semantic layer (business-meaningful names preserved) features = ['material_group', 'sales_org', 'distribution_channel', 'customer_segment', 'month_of_year', 'historical_demand_lag_4w'] target = 'confirmed_quantity' X = df[features] y = df[target] with mlflow.start_run(run_name="demand-forecast-gbr-v1"): model = GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=200, max_depth=5) model.fit(X_train, y_train) mlflow.log_metric("rmse", rmse) mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "demand_forecast_model", registered_model_name="sap_demand_forecast")

Key advantage of BDC data: The material_group, sales_org, and customer_segment columns retain their SAP business meaning from the Datasphere semantic layer. Without BDC, these would typically arrive as cryptic SAP technical codes (e.g. MATKL, VKORG) requiring additional transformation before use in ML feature engineering.

5. Data quality prerequisite checklist

BDC amplifies your SAP data — quality problems at source become quality problems in the lakehouse and in AI outputs. Before adopting BDC, validate:

  • Material master: classification completeness > 95%, no duplicate materials (check with MDG deduplication report)
  • Customer master: address standardization complete, duplicate business partner rate < 2%
  • Financial postings: period-end closing rates consistently > 98%, no large volumes of parked documents
  • Historical data depth: minimum 24 months of transactional data for meaningful ML training

Summary

  • BDC operates across three layers: Datasphere (semantic/governance), Databricks (compute/ML), SAP Analytics Cloud (analytical UX)
  • Design one Datasphere Space per business domain with its own replication flows, business layer, and data access controls
  • The BDC-Databricks connector preserves SAP business-meaningful column names in Delta Lake — eliminating the need for semantic reconstruction in the lakehouse
  • SAP Content Packages provide pre-built, governed data products for Finance, Supply Chain, HR, and Procurement
  • Validate MDG data quality before adopting BDC — set quantitative thresholds for completeness and uniqueness

Have you implemented Datasphere or are you evaluating SAP Business Data Cloud? I'd welcome technical questions and experiences in the comments — particularly around the Databricks connector configuration and content package adoption.

entry.title: Start remote Process/Actions in BTP ABAP via Task Chains from SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Modern cloud architectures allow for versatile and open interoperability. So does SAP Datasphere and SAP BTP ABAP, too. This example outlines some essentials how Datasphere can kick-off actions defined on the ABAP Cloud side, beyond system boundaries.

Starting a Task/Process in BTP ABAP remotely from SAP Datasphere using Task Chains opens up new scenarios for interoperability and integration. The goal is to orchestrate remote tasks initiated by SAP Datasphere — securely, programmatically, and fully automated.

We will cover the below points:

  • Creating a Task Chain in Datasphere including an API Call to ABAP Cloud
  • Connection in Datasphere to ABAP Cloud (for direct API call)
  • Setting up Communication System & Arrangement in ABAP Cloud
  • Executing the Task Chain in Datasphere: Trigger actions in ABAP Cloud

 

1 Architecture Overview

Architecture

The SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Task: Datasphere acts as the caller. ABAP Cloud exposes an endpoint (inbound Web Api) to trigger an action.

Option (2) and (3) complete the picture of possible solution approaches. Main differences as well as pros & cons are outlined further down in one of the last sections.

The Solution Architecture displays 3 different options:

  • (1) Use Direct API Integration 
    • Straightforward
    • Minimal cost and complexity
    • Perfect for nightly batch orchestration, event-driven data loads, or simple API-driven analytical data refresh
  • (2) Integration Suite
    • Integration involves complex message transformations, protocol mediation, multiple target systems, or when centralized API governance and monitoring across the enterprise are required.
    • Especially appropriate where iFlow reusability justifies the additional cost and setup effort or when integration suite is already in place and a common tool.
  • (3) CAP Application
    • Custom application logic is needed beyond what a direct API call/iFlow can provide
    • E.g. multi-source data aggregation, advanced parsing, enrichments
    • Additional persistence required
    • Well-suited for reusable microservice-style integrations on BTP

 

2 Prerequisites

You need

  1. SAP Datasphere tenant with developer + admin access
  2. Task Chain to design the flow of events
  3. SAP BTP ABAP Environment (ABAP Cloud) with developer access
  4. BTP ABAP: Authorization for OAuth clients, Communication Arrangements, ABAP development of Web APIs

 

 3 Creating Communication Artifacts in ABAP Cloud

Navigation: ABAP Environment Web Access → Communication Management → Communication Systems/Arrangements

Communication Arrangement

 

Communication Arrangement

Communication System

Communication System 1

Communication/Connection User:

Create a User for Inbound Communication and use the same in the subsequent step on Datasphere level for the creation of a generic HTTP connection.

 

4 ABAP Cloud Artifacts

For the sake of simplicity I won't go into the details of all of the development artifacts and configuration settings.

For sure, you will create:

  • Inbound Service
  • Service Binding
  • Communication Scenario BTP ABAP
  • Service Definition
  • Entity
  • Class implementation

An example of an action definition processData (ZI_DEMO_SERVICE):

managed implementation in class zbp_i_demo_service unique;

define behavior for ZI_DEMO_SERVICE alias DemoService
persistent table zdemoservice
lock master
{
  field ( readonly ) Id;
  field ( numbering : managed ) Id;

  create;
  update;
  delete;

  action processData result [1] zdemoservice;

  mapping for zdemoservice
  {
    Id = id;
    Name = name;
    Description = description;
    Status = status;
    CreatedBy = created_by;
    CreatedAt = created_at;
    LastChangedBy = last_changed_by;
    LastChangedAt = last_changed_at;
    LocalLastChanged = local_last_changed;
  }
}

An example of an ABAP Class with an action implementation:

CLASS lhc_DemoService DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
 
    PRIVATE SECTION.
 
    METHODS processData FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION DemoService~processData result result.
 
ENDCLASS.
 
CLASS lhc_DemoService IMPLEMENTATION.
  
  METHOD processData.
    " Your business logic implementation her
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Service Definition:

@EndUserText.label: 'Demo Service Definition'
define service ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE {
  expose ZC_DEMO_SERVICE as DemoService;
}

Inbound Service:

Communication Scenario:

 

5 Create a Connection

The first step is creating a generic HTTP connection to the endpoint exposed by ABAP Cloud. In order to do so, consider the following details. Take the connection host from the BTP ABAP instance key. The credentials must be taken from the connection user as defined in the communication arrangement.

 

An alternative to Username/Password is an OAuth based authentication mechanism. 

 

6 Creating a Task Chain in SAP Datasphere

The first step is defining a Task Chain that encapsulates the processing logic you want to execute .

In the given example:

  • The Task Chain starts with a API Task
  • Sends an email in case of success

Task Chain

In the API Task details, define the following:

  • Method POST
  • API Path - the path to the service endpoint of your ABAP Web API that you want to trigger
  • Mode Synchronous

API Task Details 1

 

Make sure to fetch the CSRF Token. The URL which I've used is simply the $metadata path of the service endpoint:

https://<ABAP_HOST>.abap.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE_O4/$metadata

 

7 Execution Results

The Task Chain in Datasphere successfully executed the ABAP Web API call:

Task Chain API successfully executed

For monitoring and troubleshooting purpose, individual API calls can be traced from BTP ABAP side. The HTTP trace can be turned on for granular levels.

 

8 Solution Options - Comparisons

The following comparison outlines the key aspects per solution/architecture option as depicted in the initial architecture diagram. I have prototyped all of the below and all options are working flawlessly. Especially w.r.t. to the CAP based solution, there is a wide open room to play with.

 

Option

Pros (+)

Cons (-)

(1) Direct API

  • Simplest setup & fewest components & lowest cost
  • No middleware latency
  • Lightweight and developer-friendly
  • Limited transformation capability
  • Less suitable for multi-step or conditional routing logic

(2) Integration Suite

  • Supports complex transformations, multiple protocols and error handling in iFlows
  • Centralized API governance via API Management
  • Recommended for complex mediated flows
  • Two-step connectivity required (Integration Suite + Datasphere)
  • Additional licensing cost
  • Higher setup and maintenance effort

(3) CAP CF App

  • Maximum flexibility for custom logic (parsing, filtering, enrichment, retry)
  • CAP integrates natively with HANA Cloud/Datasphere HDI
  • Supports secure credential management
  • Reusable microservice pattern
  • Requires application development and BTP CF deployment skills
  • Higher operational overhead
  • More BTP resource consumption
  • CAP app lifecycle must be managed independently

 

9 Conclusion/Outlook & Further aspects:

Overview of the major building blocks:

Capability

Result

No hardcoded secrets

via Communication Arrangements/System

Remote orchestration

Creating an end-to-end orchestrated chain of tasks, bidirectional if required

Cloud-native ABAP

Using appropriate ABAP Class APIs

Datasphere automation

Using Task Chain API

 

Use Cases that the implementation enables:

  • Nightly batch orchestration
  • Event-driven data loads
  • Cross-system workflows (this was our ultimate focus)
  • API-driven (transactional/analytical) data refresh

Limitations:

  • The Task Chain/API Task can't be parameterized dynamically with runtime parameters to e.g. initiate calls that have a variable payload in header or body
  • Moreover, the write-back/storing of HTTP responses is limited too

References:

  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Documentation:  https://api.sap.com/api/DatasphereTasks/overview
  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain Help Page: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/c8a54ee704e94e15926551293243fd1d/274f2736465c4c48a091c67588...

This blog entry complements my other blog on Triggering SAP Datasphere/BDC Task Chains from SAP BTP ABAP Cloud using the Task Chain REST API.

In case of questions feel free to raise them and please leave a comment if you found something incorrect - thanks for any help on that. I'm hoping this blog can support somebody out there and may facilitate some problem solving.

entry.title: Business Data Architecture: Artifact Roles - Controlled Execution Units
entry.content:
From transformations to platform implementation

In the previous post, we defined transformations as explicit, classified changes that specify what logic belongs where - which modeling layers and data product types each transformation can execute in. Transformations make architectural boundaries enforceable.

But transformations are logical definitions, not platform implementations. When you implement transformations using platform objects, a critical challenge emerges:

Scenario: The troubleshooting nightmare

It's month-end closing. Revenue numbers don't match between the executive dashboard and detailed report. You open the Revenue Data Product and find ten artifacts with technical names like "VW_REVENUE_CALC", "TF_METRICS_PROC", "TBL_REV_STAGING". Which contains the error? The names give technical hints but reveal nothing about purpose. You spend thirty minutes reading SQL across multiple objects just to understand the data flow. The clock is ticking.

This is the core problem: every analytics platform provides technical objects - SAP Datasphere has Views and Transformation Flows, Snowflake has Views and Stored Procedures, Databricks has Notebooks and Delta Tables. But the technical object type tells you the technology, not what it's meant to do. The purpose is completely implicit.

Without explicit purpose, business logic leaks into objects meant for simple composition, data products bypass controlled access points, and when something breaks you waste critical time reading implementations just to understand what each object does.

Introducing artifact roles

The missing piece is explicit purpose labeling. The solution is to define artifacts not by their technology, but by their role in the architecture.

With artifact roles, that same Revenue Data Product would be immediately readable: two Source artifacts (bringing in data), two Transformation artifacts (one for Currency Conversion, one for Revenue Recognition), and one Wrapper artifact (the controlled access point). You'd know instantly that the error has to be in one of the two Transformation artifacts - the business logic layer. Two objects to check instead of ten. Problem found in five minutes instead of thirty.

 

An artifact role has a clearly defined purpose and a set of enforceable constraints. When you see an artifact labeled with a specific role, you immediately know two things: what it does, and what it's not allowed to do. This clarity accelerates delivery - developers immediately understand where logic belongs, reducing rework and eliminating time spent deciphering hidden logic across solutions.

Artifact Roles Explained

The architecture defines artifact roles that organize into five functional categories. This categorization reflects how data products are built - from data movement through logic execution to storage and orchestration:

  • Data Boundaries (Source, Wrapper) - Control how data enters and exits data products
  • Data Transformation (Transformation, Combine) - Define how data changes within data products
  • Business Semantics (Dimension, Semantic Model) - Provide business context and analytical structure
  • Data Materialization (Persist, Buffer) - Manage data materialization and availability
  • Workflow Orchestration (Orchestration) - Coordinate when and how artifacts run

Let's explore each role in detail, understanding its purpose, constraints, and platform implementations:

 

Platform implementation guide

The table below translates each artifact role into the specific platform objects developers create. This is your implementation reference: when building a Source artifact in Snowflake, use External Table, Table, or View. When building a Transformation artifact in Databricks, use Notebook or Lakeflow Pipeline.

Use this mapping to move from architectural concepts to actual implementation - ensuring consistent structure across platforms while leveraging each platform's native capabilities. This table is designed for daily reference as you build data products.

Note: Some platform objects serve dual roles - Databricks Lakeflow Pipeline can function as Transformation (defining a single data flow) or Orchestration (coordinating dependencies across artifacts).

 

Why consistent artifact roles matter across platforms

Organizations running multi-platform data architectures - SAP Datasphere alongside Snowflake, Databricks, or Microsoft Fabric - face a fragmentation problem. Without consistent artifact roles, each platform develops its own vocabulary and patterns. Teams spend energy translating between platforms instead of building solutions.

When artifact roles are consistent across platforms, architectural boundaries remain enforceable regardless of technology. A Transformation artifact in SAP Datasphere has the same purpose, constraints, and position in the data flow as a Transformation artifact in Snowflake. The platform objects differ (Graphical View vs. SQL View), but the role and its rules are identical.

This consistency delivers strategic advantages:

  • Predictable cross-platform composition: When a Snowflake Analytical Data Product consumes a Datasphere Metric Data Product, both teams know the connection happens via Wrapper artifacts. The pattern is the same regardless of platform direction.
  • Unified governance: Architects review data products across all platforms using the same criteria - are Transformation artifacts executing only allowed patterns? Are Combine artifacts free of business logic? The rules don't change by platform.
  • Platform independence: Organizations can adopt new platforms or migrate between them without redefining their architectural principles. The roles remain constant even as technology choices evolve.

Without this consistency, multi-platform organizations fragment into platform silos where each team operates under different rules. With it, platforms remain distinct in implementation but unified in architecture.

Platform-specific optimization within role boundaries

While artifact roles remain consistent across platforms, each platform optimizes implementation using native capabilities. Snowflake excels at SQL-based transformations, Databricks at Python/ML workloads, Fabric at Power BI integration. The roles define WHAT each artifact does; platforms choose HOW based on their strengths.

What artifact roles deliver

Role-based artifacts transform how analytics teams work day-to-day:

  • Common language across teams: Developers across different teams and platforms immediately understand each other's work. A Transformation artifact means the same thing whether you're in the Datasphere team or the Snowflake team - no translation required.
  • Instant understanding: Source artifacts bring data in, Transformation artifacts execute business logic, Combine artifacts join results. Roles make purpose visible without reading implementation code.
  • Easier to maintain others' work: When you inherit a data product built by someone else, role-based structure means you immediately understand what each artifact does and where to make changes. No time wasted reverse-engineering someone else's approach.
  • Enforced boundaries: Data products must connect via Wrapper artifacts. Internal artifact access is prohibited by definition - violations are immediately visible in code review.

Up next: Data Product Patterns

You now understand artifact roles - the nine building blocks with explicit purposes and constraints. But one critical question remains: How do you ensure artifacts are combined correctly every time a data product is built?

When building a Revenue Metric Data Product, which artifacts are required? Which are optional? In what sequence should transformations execute? Should currency conversion happen before or after revenue recognition logic?

That's where Data Product Patterns come in - composition blueprints that define how artifacts combine to build complete, compliant data products. Patterns ensure every Revenue Metric Data Product is structured the same way, with transformations executing in validated sequences that maintain data integrity.

This blog post is part of the Business Data Architecture for Modern Analytics Landscapes series.

⬅️ Previous Post | Next Post ➡️

entry.title: Connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere Using SQL View Gateway
entry.content:
Connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere Using SQL View Gateway

This is the third and final post in a series on SQL View Gateway. The first post introduced SQL View Gateway and explained why it exists. The second post covered end-to-end configuration. This post shows how to connect SQL View Gateway to SAP Datasphere — and what to do with the data once it arrives there.

SAP Datasphere can consume Commerce data from SQL View Gateway in several ways. This post walks through the full integration — from prerequisites to live data in Datasphere — and covers three ingestion approaches in detail.

One important note before we start: the delta replication approach described in this post is a proof of concept, not an official SAP recommendation. It is one way to solve the problem of incremental data synchronization, and it works — but there are other valid approaches, and your implementation may look different depending on your requirements and Datasphere expertize.

Prerequisites

This post assumes SQL View Gateway is already up and running. Specifically, you will need:

  • A provisioned, RUNNING SQL View Gateway in Cloud Portal (see the previous post)
  • A deployed role with the Commerce types you want to bring into Datasphere
  • A credential assigned to that role — this is what the Data Provisioning Agent will use to authenticate
  • An SAP Datasphere tenant

Architecture overview

The integration follows a straightforward path:

  SAP Commerce Cloud
  (SQL View Gateway)
          │ SQL / TLS 1.2+
          ▼
  SAP BTP Data Provisioning Agent (dpAgent)
          │
          ▼
  SAP Datasphere
          │
          ▼
  SAP Analytics Cloud

The Data Provisioning Agent (dpAgent) is the bridge. It is a lightweight application that you deploy and run yourself — either in a cloud environment or on a server in your internal network. It connects to SQL View Gateway using standard SQL credentials and makes the data available to SAP Datasphere as a remote source.

Where you run the dpAgent determines your connectivity model:

  • Cloud-hosted dpAgent (e.g. a VM in Azure) — connects to SQL View Gateway over the public internet; use public access mode on the gateway
  • On-premise or VPN-connected dpAgent — connects through your VPN tunnel; use VPN mode on the gateway

Either approach works. The choice depends on your network architecture and security requirements.

Step 1: Set up the Data Provisioning Agent

Setting up the dpAgent is covered in the SAP Datasphere documentation. At a high level:

  1. Create a Data Provisioning Agent entry in SAP Datasphere: go to System → Configuration → Data Integration and add a new agent. Note the agent token — you will need it during agent setup.
  2. Download and install the dpAgent on your chosen host.
  3. Start the agent and register it with your Datasphere tenant using the token from step 1.
  4. Verify the agent appears as connected in Datasphere.

Once the agent is running, two network requirements must be met:

  • The dpAgent host must be able to reach SQL View Gateway — ensure there are no firewall rules on the dpAgent host or its network that block outbound SQL traffic to the gateway endpoint.
  • SQL View Gateway must allow the dpAgent's IP — in Cloud Portal, update the gateway's allowed IPs to include the IP address from which the dpAgent will connect (its public IP if connecting over the internet, or its internal IP if connecting through VPN). This was covered in detail in the previous post.

Step 2: Create a connection in SAP Datasphere

With the dpAgent running, create a connection that points to your SQL View Gateway:

  1. In SAP Datasphere, go to Connections and create a new connection.
  2. Select Microsoft SQL Server as the connection type (SQL View Gateway exposes a standard SQL Server endpoint).
  3. Enter the connection details from Cloud Portal:
    • Host: the server name from the connection string
    • Port: as shown in Cloud Portal (typically 1433)
    • Database: the database name from the connection string
    • Username and Password: the credential you created in Cloud Portal
  4. Select the dpAgent you registered in Step 1.
  5. Test the connection and save.

If the connection test fails, work through the connectivity troubleshooting checklist from the previous post — the most common causes are allowed IP misconfiguration and DNS resolution issues.

Step 3: Choose your ingestion approach

Once the connection is established, you have three options for how to consume Commerce data in Datasphere. The right choice depends on your use case.

Option 1: Remote Tables (real-time)

Remote Tables give you a live view of Commerce data — queries run directly against SQL View Gateway on demand, with no data copied into Datasphere. This is the simplest option to set up.

In SAP Datasphere, go to Data Builder → Import Remote Tables, select your SQL View Gateway connection, and choose the views you want to expose. Datasphere creates a remote table for each view. You can use these tables directly in views, models, and stories.

Best for: dashboards that need up-to-date data, low-volume queries, prototyping.

Trade-off: every query hits SQL View Gateway. Under heavy load or complex queries, this puts pressure on the gateway — consider gateway sizing carefully, and keep in mind that a dedicated gateway per use case (as described in the previous post) isolates this load from other consumers.

Option 2: Full replication (Data Flows)

Data Flows copy Commerce data into local tables in Datasphere on a scheduled basis. After the initial load, queries run against Datasphere's own storage — no ongoing load on the gateway.

In Data Builder, create a Data Flow that reads from a remote table and writes to a local table. Schedule it to run at whatever frequency your use case requires.

Best for: bulk analytical workloads, historical trend analysis, scenarios where query performance matters more than data freshness.

Trade-off: data is only as fresh as the last scheduled run. For large tables, full reloads can be expensive — which is where delta replication helps.

Option 3: Delta replication

Delta replication copies only the records that have changed since the last run. This is significantly more efficient than full replication for large, frequently updated tables — but it requires more setup.

The approach described here uses creationtime and modifiedtime columns — which are present on every SAP Commerce type — to detect new and updated records. It is one possible implementation, not the only way to approach this problem.

One important limitation: this mechanism does not detect deleted records. A record replicated to Datasphere will remain there permanently, even if it is subsequently deleted in Commerce. Depending on your use case, this may or may not be acceptable. Handling deletions requires additional implementation on the Commerce side — for example, soft-deleting records by marking them with a flag rather than removing them from the database, and propagating that flag through the replication. The right approach depends on your requirements and is outside the scope of this post.

Setting up delta replication

How the mechanism works

The delta replication is driven by two independent clocks that work together.

The DeltaImports table acts as a synchronization manifest. Each row represents one data source to replicate. The run column holds a counter — it tracks how many times this source should have been synchronized. You control how often a source is synchronized by controlling how often this counter is incremented. A Data Flow in Datasphere handles the incrementing on a schedule you define per source.

The HANA scheduler job runs independently at a fixed interval (for example, every 10 minutes). On each run, it scans the DeltaImports table and compares each row's run value against the last recorded run in delta_import_control. If run is higher than last_run, it means at least one synchronisation is overdue — so it replicates all records created or modified since the last run, then updates last_run to match.

This two-clock design means synchronization frequency is controlled by how often you increment run, but the actual replication happens at the HANA scheduler's cadence. In practice, data will be at most one scheduler interval late relative to the run counter. For example: if a Data Flow increments run every 15 minutes, and the HANA job runs every 10 minutes, data will be synchronized approximately every 15 minutes — but never more than 10 minutes late relative to when the increment happened.

This also means you can replicate different sources at different frequencies simply by scheduling their Data Flows differently — without changing the HANA job at all.

One practical implication for sizing your scheduler interval: data will never be fresher than the HANA job schedule, regardless of how frequently you increment run. If the HANA job runs every 10 minutes, data will be at most 10 minutes stale — incrementing run every minute will not change that. Set the HANA job schedule to the maximum acceptable data latency for your use case, and treat it as the ceiling on data freshness for the entire delta replication setup.

At the same time, avoid setting the interval too aggressively. Each run of the HANA job executes queries and upsert operations against your Datasphere schema — running it every few seconds would introduce unnecessary load on the HANA side. Delta replication is designed for scheduled synchronization scenarios where data latency of minutes is acceptable, not seconds. If you need truly real-time access to Commerce data, Remote Tables are the better fit.

Overview

The mechanism works as follows:

  1. A control table in Datasphere tracks the last successful synchronization timestamp per data source.
  2. A scheduled job runs at regular intervals and upserts records that have been created or modified since the last run.
  3. A history table records each run for auditing and troubleshooting.

3.1 Create the supporting tables in SAP Datasphere

In SAP Datasphere, go to Configuration → Database Access → Database Analysis Users and create a user with Space Schema Access enabled. Open Database Explorer and connect with this user.

Creating a Database Analysis User automatically creates a corresponding Open SQL Schema — a dedicated schema in the underlying HANA database where you can create tables, views, and procedures. The schema name is derived from the username and will look something like USERNAME#SCHEMANAME. In the code snippets throughout this section, we use CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE as a placeholder — replace it with the actual schema name shown in your Database Explorer after creating the user.

In the Open SQL Schema, create the following tables:

Control table — tracks the last successful run per data source:

create column table delta_import_control (
      pk BIGINT primary key,
      last_datetime TIMESTAMP not null,
      last_run BIGINT
  );

History table — append-only log of every run:

create column table delta_import_history (
      pk BIGINT primary key generated always as identity,
      delta_import BIGINT not null,
      run_datetime TIMESTAMP not null,
      run_from TIMESTAMP,
      run_to TIMESTAMP not null,
      affected BIGINT not null,
      foreign key (delta_import) references delta_import_control
  );

3.2 Create the DeltaImports entity in Datasphere

The synchronization mechanism is driven by a table called DeltaImports that lives in your Datasphere space. It tells the system which source views to sync and where to put the results.

Create a local table called DeltaImports with the following structure (use the CSN below in Data Builder):

{
    "definitions": {
      "DeltaImports": {
        "kind": "entity",
        "elements": {
          "PK": { "type": "cds.Integer64", "key": true, "notNull": true },
          "src": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 128, "notNull": true },
          "res": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 128, "notNull": true },
          "run": { "type": "cds.Integer64", "notNull": true, "default": { "val": "0" } }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  • src — fully qualified name of the source view in Datasphere (e.g. "MYSPACE"."V_Orders")
  • res — fully qualified name of the target local table (e.g. "CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE"."Orders") — replace with your Open SQL Schema name
  • run — a counter incremented on each run; used to trigger the Data Flow

Then create a 1:1 view on DeltaImports called V_DeltaImports and mark it Exposed for analytics. This view is what the scheduled Data Flow reads.

3.3 Create the HANA procedures

Back in Database Explorer, create the following stored procedures in your Open SQL Schema.

prepare_incremental_import — called once per data source to create the target local table and grant the necessary permissions to your Datasphere space:

create or replace procedure prepare_incremental_import(
      in src NVARCHAR(128),
      in res NVARCHAR(128)
  ) as
  begin
      declare sch nvarchar(128);
      select CURRENT_SCHEMA into sch from dummy;

      exec 'create row table "' || :sch || '"."' || :res || '" as (select * from ' || :src || ')';
      exec 'alter table "' || :sch || '"."' || :res || '" add primary key ("PK")';

      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'SELECT',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'UPDATE',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'DELETE',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'INSERT',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
  end;

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID with your actual Datasphere space ID.

incremental_import — the core synchronization procedure. For a given source/target pair, it upserts all records modified or created since the last run:

create or replace procedure incremental_import(
      in pk bigint,
      in src NVARCHAR(128),
      in res NVARCHAR(128),
      in run bigint
  ) as
  begin
      declare dt, curr_dt timestamp;
      declare query nvarchar(2000);
      declare affected bigint;

      select UTCTOLOCAL(CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP, 'Etc/UTC') into curr_dt from dummy;
      select ifnull((select last_datetime from delta_import_control where pk = :pk), null)
          into dt from dummy;

      query = 'upsert ' || :res || ' SELECT * from ' || :src
          || ' where "modifiedtime" < ''' || :curr_dt || ''''
          || ' and "creationtime" < ''' || :curr_dt || '''';

      IF dt is not null THEN
          query = query || ' and ("modifiedtime" > ''' || :dt
              || ''' or "creationtime" > ''' || :dt || ''')';
      END IF;

      exec query;
      SELECT ::ROWCOUNT INTO affected from dummy;

      upsert delta_import_control(pk, last_datetime, last_run)
          values (:pk, :curr_dt, :run) WITH PRIMARY KEY;
      insert into delta_import_history(delta_import, run_datetime, run_from, run_to, affected)
          values (:pk, :curr_dt, :dt, :curr_dt, :affected);
  end;

incremental_imports — the orchestrator. Reads V_DeltaImports and calls incremental_import for each entry that has a new run value:

create or replace procedure incremental_imports as
  begin
      DECLARE pk, run BIGINT;
      DECLARE src, res NVARCHAR(128);
      DECLARE deltas table (
          pk bigint, src nvarchar(128), res nvarchar(128),
          run bigint, last_run bigint
      );
      DECLARE CURSOR c_deltas for select pk, src, res, run, last_run from :deltas;

      deltas =
          select di.pk, di."src", di."res", di."run", dic.last_run
          from "YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_DeltaImports" di
          left outer join delta_import_control dic on di.pk = dic.pk;

      FOR delta AS c_deltas DO
          if :delta.last_run is null or :delta.run > :delta.last_run then
              call incremental_import(:delta.pk, :delta.src, :delta.res, :delta.run);
          end if;
      END FOR;
  end;

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID with your actual space ID.

3.4 Schedule the synchronization

Create a scheduler job in HANA to run incremental_imports at your desired interval. The example below runs every 10 minutes:

CREATE SCHEDULER JOB do_incremental_imports
      CRON '* * * * * * 00,10,20,30,40,50'
      ENABLE PROCEDURE incremental_imports;

Adjust the schedule to match your freshness requirements and gateway capacity.

3.5 Register a data source for delta replication

For each Commerce type you want to replicate using this mechanism:

  1. In Data Builder, use Import Remote Tables to create a remote table for the SQL View Gateway view (e.g. salesreporting_order).
  2. Create a 1:1 view on that remote table (e.g. V_Orders) and mark it Exposed for analytics.
  3. In Database Explorer, call prepare_incremental_import to create the target table and grant permissions:
CALL prepare_incremental_import(
      '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"',
      'Orders'
  );
  • Insert a row into DeltaImports to register this source:
INSERT INTO "YOUR_SPACE_ID"."DeltaImports"(PK, src, res, run)
  VALUES (
      1,
      '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"',
      '"CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE"."Orders"', -- replace with your Open SQL Schema name
      0
  );

  • Create a Data Flow to trigger the synchronization.

    This Data Flow serves one specific purpose: it increments the run counter in DeltaImports for a given source. That increment is the signal that tells incremental_imports to process this source on the next scheduler run.

    The Data Flow has three nodes:

    • Source: reads from the DeltaImports local table
    • Projection: filters to the row where src matches your source view, and adds a calculated column run_inc = run + 1
    • Target: writes back to DeltaImports using upsert mode (update if exists, insert if not)

    To create it in Data Builder:

    1. Create a new Data Flow.
    2. Add DeltaImports as a source node.
    3. Add a Projection node after the source. In the projection:
      • Add a filter: "src" = '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"'
      • Map all columns through unchanged (PK, src, res)
      • Add a calculated column run_inc with expression "run" + 1
    4. Add DeltaImports as a target node. Set the write mode to Upsert. Map run_inc → run, and all other columns through unchanged.
    5. Save and activate the Data Flow.

    The JSON below shows a complete example for OrderEntries — you can import it directly into Data Builder and adapt it for your source:

{
    "dataflows": {
      "DF_OrderEntries": {
        "kind": "sap.dis.dataflow",
        "contents": {
          "processes": {
            "source1": {
              "component": "com.sap.database.table.consumer",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "DeltaImports",
                "config": {
                  "service": "HANA",
                  "hanaConnection": { "configurationType": "Configuration Manager", "connectionID": "$DWC" },
                  "qualifiedName": "DeltaImports",
                  "dwcEntity": "DeltaImports"
                }
              }
            },
            "projection1": {
              "component": "com.sap.dataflow.projection",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "Projection 1",
                "config": {
                  "expression": "\"src\" = '\"YOUR_SPACE_ID\".\"V_Orders\"'",
                  "attributeMappings": [
                    { "target": "PK", "expression": "\"PK\"" },
                    { "target": "src", "expression": "\"src\"" },
                    { "target": "res", "expression": "\"res\"" },
                    { "target": "run_inc", "expression": "\"run\" + 1" }
                  ]
                }
              }
            },
            "target1": {
              "component": "com.sap.database.table.producer",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "DeltaImports",
                "config": {
                  "service": "HANA",
                  "hanaConnection": { "configurationType": "Configuration Manager", "connectionID": "$DWC" },
                  "qualifiedName": "DeltaImports",
                  "dwcEntity": "DeltaImports",
                  "mode": "append",
                  "upsert": true,
                  "attributeMappings": [
                    { "expression": "\"PK\"", "target": "PK" },
                    { "expression": "\"src\"", "target": "src" },
                    { "expression": "\"res\"", "target": "res" },
                    { "expression": "\"run_inc\"", "target": "run" }
                  ]
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID and V_Orders with your actual space ID and source view name. Create a separate Data Flow for each Commerce type you are replicating.

Repeat steps 1–5 for each Commerce type you want to replicate.

Step 4: Model your data in Datasphere

Once your Commerce data is flowing into Datasphere — whether via Remote Tables, Data Flows, or delta replication — you can start building your data model.

The typical pattern for Commerce analytics:

  • Fact tables: transactional data — Orders, OrderEntries, ReturnRequests
  • Dimension tables: reference data — Products, Categories, Customers
  • Analytical models: join facts and dimensions into consumption views

This is standard Datasphere modeling and is covered in the SAP Datasphere documentation.

Step 5: Connect to SAP Analytics Cloud

With your data modeled in Datasphere, connecting SAP Analytics Cloud is straightforward — SAC has native connectivity to Datasphere. From there you can build stories, dashboards, and planning models on top of your Commerce data.

SAC–Datasphere integration is covered in the SAP Analytics Cloud documentation.

A few things to keep in mind

This is a proof of concept. The delta replication approach shown here is one way to solve incremental synchronization. It worked well in testing, but it is not an official SAP pattern. Your implementation may differ — for example, you might use Datasphere's built-in replication flows, a different change detection mechanism, or a third-party ETL tool. Treat the code snippets as a starting point, not a blueprint.

Data privacy and data hygiene are your responsibility. SQL View Gateway exposes raw Commerce data — it restricts access to certain internal technical types, but it does not sanitize or validate the content of the data it exposes. If your Commerce data includes values entered by end users (for example, free-text fields, addresses, or customer-supplied identifiers), treat those values as untrusted input. Validate and sanitize them before using them in downstream queries, models, or applications — SQL injection and other injection attacks are possible if user-supplied values are passed through without inspection. Separately, ensure that what you expose complies with your data processing obligations, consent requirements, and any cross-border data transfer rules applicable to your deployment.

Credential management. The dpAgent credential is a long-lived technical account. Rotate its password regularly, restrict its role to only the types needed for Datasphere, and treat the credential with the same care as any other service account.

Gateway sizing. If you use Remote Tables, every Datasphere query hits the gateway directly. Monitor gateway CPU utilization and consider a dedicated gateway for Datasphere traffic to isolate it from other consumers.

Learn more

  • SAP Datasphere documentation
  • Data Provisioning Agent documentation
  • SAP Analytics Cloud documentation
  • Business overview video
  • Configuration guide video
  • SQL View Gateway documentation

entry.title: Supporting Your SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey Through Community Discussions
entry.content:
The role of the data architect is evolving fast. It is no longer only about managing databases or designing models, it is about shaping scalable, governed, AI-ready data ecosystems that turn business strategy into real outcomes. 

SAP has recently introduced a new learning path for data architects, combining foundational architecture concepts with practical implementation topics such as data modeling, metadata, Data Mesh, Data Fabric, Data Lakehouse, SAP Business Data Cloud integration, and modern hybrid landscapes with platforms such as Snowflake, Databricks, and Google BigQuery. 

If you are following the SAP Data Architect learning journey or preparing for the certification, this community is a great place to take the next step: bring your real-world questions into practitioner discussions here. Use this thread to share what you are working through, such as: 

  • How to translate architecture principles into implementation decisions,
  • How to connect SAP Business Data Cloud with existing SAP and non-SAP landscapes, 
  • How to approach governance, metadata, ownership, and data quality in practice, 
  • How to modernize BW, analytics, or data engineering scenarios, 
  • How to prepare data foundations for AI and advanced analytics. 

The Data Professionals group is designed for hands-on practitioners, data architects, data engineers, analytics engineers, business analysts, data stewards, platform leads, SAP customers, and partners, who build, integrate, model, and operationalize data and AI solutions. So, whether you are studying for certification, applying the concepts in a project, or helping others define their data architecture roadmap: join the discussion, ask your questions, and share what you’ve learned. 

What is one real-world data architecture challenge you are trying to solve right now?
